items.title: Principles for understanding trust in artificial intelligence
items.author: Madeline G. Reinecke
items.content: The growing prevalence of artificial intelligence raises questions about when, why and how people trust machines — and whether they should do so in the first place. In this Review, Everett et al. identify six principles that can structure an understanding of trust in AI and consider the ethical implications of studying trust in AI.

items.title: The explicit-Bayes hypothesis for cognition
items.author: Dobromir Rahnev
items.content: It is often asserted that human cognition is Bayesian, but that broad claim is difficult to test in a falsifiable way. We suggest that researchers specifically assess a narrower, falsifiable hypothesis: that cognition explicitly uses Bayes’ rule at the algorithmic level.

items.title: Transdiagnostic mechanisms of decreased sound tolerance
items.author: Kelly N. Jahn
items.content: Decreased sound tolerance refers to alterations in a person’s ability to cope with everyday sounds that do not bother most people. In this Review, Scheerer et al. synthesize evidence supporting that two frequent subtypes of these alterations, hyperacusis and misophonia, are transdiagnostic to psychopathology.

items.title: A children’s rights perspective to child psychological maltreatment
items.author: Lisa Marie Schaumburg
items.content: A children’s rights perspective to child psychological maltreatment

items.title: Connecting extinction learning in the laboratory and the wild
items.author: Onur Güntürkün
items.content: In laboratory extinction learning with non-human animals, later relapses or amplifications of the extinguished behaviour are difficult to explain. In this Perspective, Anselme and Güntürkün argue that behaviours that seem paradoxical in the laboratory are functionally meaningful for animals in the wild.

items.title: Advancing a dynamic approach to cultural psychology beyond stable dichotomies
items.author: Batja Mesquita
items.content: The framework of individualism versus collectivism has encouraged conceptions of culture as dichotomous, bounded, monolithic and stable. In this Review, Uskul and Mesquita describe research that moves beyond these conceptualizations of culture and considers research on a wider range of cultural variety in psychological tendencies, within-nation variation and cultural dynamics.

items.title: African participation in global psychological science
items.author: Kevin Durrheim
items.content: Dominant responses to concerns about the underrepresentation of African populations in psychological science have failed to address underlying asymmetries of power and knowledge production, and might inadvertently reinforce them. Meaningful participation in global psychological science should be grounded in epistemic self-determination, intra-African connectivity and open science infrastructures.

items.title: Psychologists must be involved in building conversational AI chatbots
items.author: Xuan Zhao
items.content: Conversational AI chatbots could democratize access to personalized learning and mental health support, but they might also exacerbate psychological risk and social isolation. Realizing their full potential requires involving psychologists in the AI industry and embedding psychological theory and empirical methods into system design, development, evaluation, and monitoring.
